section.title: The Broken Covenant
section.sectionRationale: Jeremiah is sent to remind Judah of the covenant sworn at Sinai, obey Me and you will be My people, and to announce that the nation has broken it beyond repair. Their many gods, as numerous as their cities, cannot save them, and the LORD who once called Judah a flourishing olive tree now decrees its branches will be burned.
section.thesis: The covenant Israel swore to keep at the exodus, listen to My voice, has been broken by generations of stubborn disobedience and idolatry, so that the LORD who bound Himself to bless obedience now must bring the covenant curses, yet even this judgment flows from a relationship He initiated and still calls His own.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the terms of the covenant sworn at the exodus and Israel's long history of refusing to obey them; the discovery of a conspiracy of unfaithfulness so deep that God forbids Jeremiah to pray for the people any longer; and the LORD's grieving question over His own beloved house, once a flourishing olive tree, now decreed for burning.

units.label: Listen to the words of this covenant
units.note: cursed is the man who does not obey

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD:
“Listen to the words of this {{berith|covenant}} and tell them to the men of Judah and the residents of Jerusalem.
You must tell them that this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: {{arur|Cursed}} is the man who does not obey the words of this covenant,

which I commanded your forefathers when I brought them out of the land of Egypt, out of the iron furnace, saying, ‘Obey Me, and do everything I command you, and you will be My people, and I will be your God.’

This was in order to establish the oath I swore to your forefathers, to give them a land flowing with milk and honey, as it is to this day.” 
“Amen, LORD,” I answered.
Then the LORD said to me, “Proclaim all these words in the cities of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem, saying: Hear the words of this covenant and carry them out.
For from the time I brought your fathers out of the land of Egypt until today, I strongly warned them again and again, saying, ‘Obey My voice.’

Yet they would not obey or incline their ears, but each one followed the stubbornness of his evil heart. So I brought on them all the curses of this covenant I had commanded them to follow but they did not keep.”

units.label: There is a conspiracy
units.note: do not pray for this people

verses.text: And the LORD told me, “There is a {{qesher|conspiracy}} among the men of Judah and the residents of Jerusalem.

They have returned to the sins of their forefathers who refused to obey My words. They have followed other gods to serve them. The house of Israel and the house of Judah have broken the covenant I made with their fathers.
Therefore this is what the LORD says: ‘I am about to bring upon them a disaster that they cannot escape. They will cry out to Me, but I will not listen to them.
Then the cities of Judah and the residents of Jerusalem will go and cry out to the gods to which they have been burning incense, but these gods certainly will not save them in their time of disaster.
Your gods are indeed as numerous as your cities, O Judah; the altars of shame you have set up, the altars to burn incense to Baal, are as many as the streets of Jerusalem.’
As for you, do not pray for these people. Do not raise up a cry or a prayer on their behalf, for I will not be listening when they call out to Me in their time of disaster.

units.label: What right has My beloved in My house?
units.note: the olive tree set on fire

verses.text: What right has My beloved in My house, having carried out so many evil schemes? Can consecrated meat avert your doom, so that you can rejoice?
The LORD once called you a flourishing {{zayit|olive tree}}, beautiful with well-formed fruit. But with a mighty roar He will set it on fire, and its branches will be consumed.

The LORD of Hosts, who planted you, has decreed disaster against you on account of the evil that the house of Israel and the house of Judah have brought upon themselves, provoking Me to anger by burning incense to Baal.”

berith.gloss: Covenant, a binding agreement sworn with obligations and sanctions on both sides. This is the same covenant sworn at the exodus, obey Me and you will be My people, now formally charged as broken by Judah's persistent idolatry.

arur.gloss: Cursed, the pronouncement that invokes the covenant's sanctions on one who breaks it. This exact word structures the blessings and curses of Deuteronomy 27 to 28, the legal background this whole oracle assumes.

qesher.gloss: Conspiracy, a binding together for rebellion. God uses the language of political treachery to describe Judah's collective, coordinated turn to other gods, treating covenant unfaithfulness as an act of active betrayal, not passive drift.

zayit.gloss: Olive tree, a symbol of beauty, fruitfulness, and stability in the ancient world. God once called Israel a flourishing olive tree; this same image becomes, in Romans 11, the picture of Israel's branches broken off and Gentile branches grafted in by faith.

basic.context: Jeremiah is commissioned to remind Judah of the covenant terms sworn when God brought their forefathers out of Egypt: obey Me, and you will be My people, and I will be your God. But Judah has repeated the sins of the wilderness generation, chasing other gods until, as the LORD says, there is a conspiracy of unfaithfulness among them. Their many altars to Baal, as numerous as the streets of Jerusalem, cannot save them, and the LORD forbids Jeremiah to intercede any longer.
basic.hermeneutics: This is covenant lawsuit language drawn from Deuteronomy's blessings and curses; Judah is being formally charged with breach of a real, binding relationship, not merely disappointing God in the abstract. The command not to pray for the people, echoed again in chapter 14, is a severe mercy warning of how far things have gone, not a permanent revocation of grace, since this same book later promises restoration.
basic.originalLanguages: The covenant, berith, invoked here is the same word for the Sinai relationship; those who break its terms are arur, cursed, under its sanctions. God names a qesher, conspiracy, among His own people, and mourns that the nation He once called a flourishing zayit, olive tree, now faces the fire.
basic.typology: The flourishing olive tree that Judah once was, now cut down and burned for unfaithfulness, becomes, in Paul's letter to the Romans, the image of Israel's branches broken off and wild branches grafted in by faith, showing that belonging to God's olive tree was always a matter of faithful trust, not mere physical descent.
basic.christ: Jeremiah 31, later in this same book, promises a new covenant to replace the one broken here, a covenant Jesus explicitly inaugurates at the Last Supper, this cup is the new covenant in My blood, Luke 22:20. Where Israel could not keep the covenant's terms and came under its curse, Christ perfectly obeys and becomes cursed in the disobedient's place, Galatians 3:13, so that the olive tree cut down here can, in Him, be grafted and flourish again.
basic.god: God bound Himself to Israel with real, sworn terms; His anger at their unfaithfulness is not arbitrary but the response of a covenant partner betrayed by generations of idolatry. Even His refusal to let Jeremiah intercede reveals how seriously He takes covenant loyalty. Yet the same God who decrees the olive tree's burning is the God who, later in this very book, promises a new and better covenant.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 28 lays out the same covenant blessings and curses invoked here. Romans 11:17-24 develops the olive tree image into Israel and the nations grafted together by faith. Galatians 3:10-13 explains that Christ became a curse for us, the very curse this covenant threatens. Jeremiah 31:31-34 promises the new covenant that answers this broken one.
basic.oneStory: This is the failure of the old covenant under Sinai's terms, a failure so deep God forbids intercession for it, yet the very passage that announces the broken covenant's curse sits inside a book that will later promise a new covenant written on the heart, fulfilled when Christ, cursed in our place, inaugurates that covenant in His own blood.

context.literary: This unit opens a new ten chapter block, 11:1-20:18, marked by prose narrative about Jeremiah's own life interwoven with poetic oracles, beginning and ending with threats to the prophet's own safety.
context.historical: Likely proclaimed early in Jehoiakim's reign, perhaps tied to the reforms of Josiah that preceded it, when covenant renewal had been publicly celebrated yet had not produced lasting obedience in the nation's heart.
context.canonical: This covenant lawsuit echoes Deuteronomy's own blessings and curses and anticipates the new covenant promise later in Jeremiah 31, forming a hinge between the broken old covenant and the promised new one fulfilled in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To formally charge Judah with breaking the covenant sworn at the exodus, using the legal language of covenant lawsuit to show that the coming disaster is not arbitrary but the fulfillment of terms Israel itself agreed to generations before.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical covenant and a specific verdict on Judah's breach of it; it is not a general statement that God never hears prayer. The enduring truth is that persistent, unrepentant covenant unfaithfulness has real consequences, and that God's patience, though long, is not infinite within a given generation's rebellion.

debates.question: Does God's command not to pray for the people, verse 14, mean prayer itself becomes ineffective or forbidden more broadly?

views.label: A specific, situational restriction
views.case: The command is addressed to Jeremiah alone regarding this particular generation's entrenched unfaithfulness, similar restrictions appear in 7:16 and 14:11, and the book later promises restoration and a new covenant, showing this is not God's permanent posture.

views.label: A severe warning about the point of no return
views.case: Others read this as showing that a nation, or a person, can reach a point where continued rebellion forecloses further intercession for that specific judgment, though not forever, since mercy resumes even for later generations of the same people.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this is a severe, situational judgment on a specific generation's hardened unfaithfulness, not a universal statement that prayer is powerless or forbidden for God's people generally.

study.originalLanguages: The covenant, berith, invoked here is the same binding relationship established at Sinai; to be arur, cursed, is to fall under its sworn sanctions, the same word structuring Deuteronomy's blessings and curses. God names a qesher, conspiracy, treating Judah's collective idolatry as active, coordinated betrayal rather than passive neglect, and mourns that the nation once called a flourishing zayit, olive tree, now faces the fire, an image later reused by Paul for Israel and the nations grafted together in Christ.

typology.type: The flourishing olive tree, once beautiful, now cut down and burned for unfaithfulness, Jeremiah 11:16-17
typology.fulfillment: Paul develops this into the picture of natural branches broken off and wild branches grafted in by faith, Romans 11:17-24.

typology.type: The curse of the broken covenant, Jeremiah 11:3
typology.fulfillment: Christ becomes a curse for us, redeeming those under the law's curse, Galatians 3:10-13.

study.christ: Jeremiah 31, later in this very book, promises a new covenant to replace the one broken here, a covenant Jesus explicitly inaugurates at the Last Supper, this cup is the new covenant in My blood, Luke 22:20. Where Israel could not keep the covenant's terms and came under its curse, Christ perfectly obeys and becomes cursed in the place of the disobedient, Galatians 3:13, so that the olive tree cut down and burned here can, in Him, be grafted in again and flourish, exactly the hope Paul unfolds in Romans 11.
study.god: God bound Himself to Israel with real, sworn terms at the exodus; His anger at centuries of unfaithfulness is not arbitrary but the just response of a covenant partner betrayed. His refusal to let Jeremiah intercede for this generation reveals how seriously He takes covenant loyalty, yet He remains the same God who, later in this book, promises a new and better covenant written on the heart, showing His justice and His mercy are never finally at odds.

crossReferences.note: Lays out the same covenant blessings and curses invoked in this oracle.
Develops the olive tree image into natural branches broken off and wild branches grafted in by faith.
Explains that Christ became a curse for us, redeeming those under the covenant's curse.
Promises the new covenant that answers and heals the breach announced here.

study.oneStory: This is the failure of the old covenant under Sinai's sworn terms, a failure so deep God forbids intercession for this generation's unfaithfulness, yet the very passage announcing the broken covenant's curse sits inside a book that will later promise a new covenant written on the heart, fulfilled when Christ, cursed in our place, inaugurates that covenant in His own blood.

sermon.title: Christmas at the Castle, Part 3
sermon.why: Carson reads Jeremiah 11 in full at the start of this message, showing how the covenant curses invoked at Sinai are now falling on Judah for generations of unfaithfulness, precisely the covenant lawsuit this study traces.

quiz.q: What were the original terms of the covenant God reminds Judah of in 11:3-5?

options.text: Obey Me and do everything I command, and you will be My people, and I will be your God.
options.note: Verse 4 states this plainly as the covenant's core term.

options.text: Build a temple in Jerusalem before entering the land.
options.note: Temple building is not the covenant term recalled in this passage.

options.text: Offer burnt offerings every day without exception.
options.note: Sacrifice is not the specific term recalled here; obedience to God's voice is.

options.text: Conquer all the surrounding nations immediately.
options.note: Conquest of Canaan is background history, not the covenant term restated in these verses.

quiz.q: Why does God tell Jeremiah not to pray for the people in 11:14?

options.text: Because their unfaithfulness has gone so far that He will not listen to their cries in the day of disaster.
options.note: Verse 14 states this directly as the reason.

options.text: Because God had already fully forgiven the nation and prayer was unnecessary.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the passage says; judgment, not forgiveness, is being announced.

options.text: Because Jeremiah was too weak or unqualified to intercede effectively.
options.note: The reason given concerns the people's unfaithfulness, not any deficiency in Jeremiah himself.

options.text: Because prayer had been abolished for everyone in Judah from that point on.
options.note: This overstates the command, which is specific to this people's coming disaster, not a general ban on prayer.

quiz.q: How does verse 13 describe the extent of Judah's idolatry?

options.text: They have as many gods as cities, and altars to Baal as many as the streets of Jerusalem.
options.note: Verse 13 states this exact, sweeping comparison.

options.text: They worshiped exactly twelve different gods, one for each tribe.
options.note: This specific number is not stated anywhere in the passage.

options.text: They had remained fully faithful to the LORD alone.
options.note: This directly contradicts the chapter's central charge of widespread idolatry.

options.text: They worshiped only one significant false god, Baal.
options.note: Baal is named, but the text stresses idols as numerous as their cities, not a single false god alone.

quiz.q: What image does God use for Judah in verse 16?

options.text: A flourishing olive tree, beautiful with well-formed fruit, now set on fire.
options.note: Verse 16 uses exactly this image of a once thriving tree now burned.

options.text: A cedar of Lebanon toppled by a storm.
options.note: This is not the specific tree image used in this verse.

options.text: A fortress wall that cannot be breached.
options.note: This image contradicts the passage, which pictures destruction, not impregnable strength.

options.text: A vine trampled underfoot by wild animals.
options.note: Vine and vineyard imagery appears elsewhere in Jeremiah, but the specific image used here is the olive tree.

quiz.q: What can the gods Judah has served do for them in their time of disaster, according to verse 12?

options.text: Nothing at all; they certainly will not save them.
options.note: Verse 12 states this plainly and directly.

options.text: Deliver them fully from the coming disaster.
options.note: This directly contradicts what verse 12 actually says.

options.text: Delay the coming judgment for a limited season.
options.note: No such delay or partial help is described in the text.

options.text: Provide some temporary comfort, though not full deliverance.
options.note: The text states total inability to save, with no mention of even temporary comfort.

questions: God reminds Judah of the covenant's original terms, obey Me and I will be your God. Where in your own walk with God have promises of blessing quietly become detached from a life of obedience?

God tells Jeremiah not to pray for a people so deep in unfaithfulness. Have you ever sensed that persistent, unrepentant sin was hardening a relationship, in your own life or someone else's, past easy intercession?
Judah had as many gods as cities. What competing loyalties or false securities have multiplied quietly in your own life?
God grieves over Judah, once a flourishing olive tree, now set for burning. How does picturing God's grief, and not only His anger, change the way you think about His discipline?
Christ was cursed in the place of those who could not keep the covenant. How does knowing this change the weight you feel over your own past failures to obey?

translationNotes.note: The iron furnace describes the intensity of Egyptian slavery, an image also used in Deuteronomy 4:20, forming the backdrop of the redemption that grounded Israel's covenant obligation.
The altars of shame is a term of deliberate mockery, substituting shame for the honor these altars were supposed to represent.
The olive tree image for Israel recurs in Hosea 14:6 and becomes foundational for Paul's extended argument about Israel and the nations in Romans 11.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit and sermon link verified via live web fetch this session confirming Don Carson's sermon covering Jeremiah 11 to 15 at thegospelcoalition.org.
